title: Pre-tupping ewe management
subtitle: Getting ewes into the right condition and health before the ram goes in.
excerpt: The six weeks before the ram goes in largely determines conception rate and litter size for the coming lambing. This covers target body condition, flushing strategy, booster vaccinations, worm treatment, and the pre-tupping tasks that pay the most reliably in a small flock.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb, tupping management guidance. DEFRA Welfare Code for Sheep (2023). SAC Consulting Scotland flock health calendar.

glossaryTerms.definition: Moving ewes onto improving pasture or adding a concentrate supplement in the three to four weeks before the ram is introduced. Rising nutrition at this stage increases ovulation rate and often improves twin and triplet rates.

A multivalent vaccine protecting against clostridial diseases (pulpy kidney, lamb dysentery, tetanus, braxy, blackleg). Annual booster in the ewe three to five weeks before lambing passes passive immunity to lambs via colostrum.
Mating season for sheep. The ram is introduced to the ewes and natural service takes place. In the UK, most lowland breeds cycle from September to January.

content.text: The period from weaning (July) to the introduction of the ram (typically October for lowland breeds) is the time to rebuild ewe condition after the demands of lactation.
ewes in poor condition produces lower conception rates and smaller litters. The work done in late September and October is the most directly leveraged investment in a lambing season.
Six weeks before the planned ram introduction date, work through the flock and
. Separate ewes scoring below 2.5 and feed them concentrates individually to bring them up before tupping. Ewes scoring 4 or above need tighter grazing management rather than more feed.
Move ewes onto the best-quality pasture or rested aftermath grass three to four weeks before the ram goes in.

works by the rising plane of nutrition stimulating a higher ovulation rate. For a very small flock on dry autumn pasture, supplementing with 200 g of concentrate per ewe per day achieves the same effect. The key is that nutrition is rising when the ram goes in, not declining.

four to six weeks before tupping. A high worm burden suppresses the immune response and reduces fertility. Use the appropriate wormer class based on your monitoring; rotate class annually to limit resistance. Record the product and date in the holding register.

booster, do it at this stage rather than in late pregnancy to reduce the number of handling sessions. The timing of the late-pregnancy booster (four to six weeks before lambing) is fixed; this pre-tupping handling is a good opportunity for any annual treatments that are otherwise separately scheduled.

While the ewes are being managed, check the ram: body condition, feet, teeth (a ram with bad teeth may struggle to hold condition when put to work), and testicles (both present, equal size, firm). A ram showing lameness or poor condition at this stage needs remediation before the season starts, not during it.
